planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), fertile soil in full sun to part shade; red jaboticaba (Plinia trunciflora) is a smaller, thinner-skinned, and slightly sweeter jaboticaba species than the commonly grown P. cauliflora, bearing red to deep-red-purple fruits directly on the trunk in the characteristic jaboticaba fashion; slightly more cold-tolerant than some jaboticaba species.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; jaboticabas are slow-growing and may take 5–8 years to first fruit but eventually produce multiple crops per year. Plinia trunciflora (red jaboticaba or jaboticaba vermelha) is one of several species in the jaboticaba group of Brazilian native fruit trees — distinct from the commonly cultivated Plinia cauliflora (the dark-purple or black jaboticaba) by producing smaller fruits that ripen red to deep ruby-red rather than purple-black, with thinner skin, slightly sweeter flavor, and a somewhat shorter delay before first flowering after planting in good conditions. Like all jaboticabas, P. trunciflora produces its fruits cauliflourously — directly on the bark of the trunk and main branches, covering the tree's lower structure with dense clusters of round, grape-like fruits that appear to erupt directly from the wood, creating one of the most visually spectacular fruiting displays in subtropical horticulture. The fruits are eaten fresh or made into wine, liqueur, and jam in Brazil; they are thin-skinned enough that the entire fruit (including the somewhat tannic skin) can be popped into the mouth. Jaboticabas produce multiple crops per year in warm, humid conditions. P. trunciflora is noted as marginally more cold-tolerant than some jaboticaba relatives, making it a candidate for protected zone 9 microclimates.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Jaboticaba
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Myrciaria and Plinia
California Rare Fruit Growers — Jaboticaba Species

common_name: Cedar Bay Cherry (Beach Cherry, Reinwardtiana Berry)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy coastal soil in full sun to part shade; cedar bay cherry is an Australian native Eugenia (related to Surinam cherry) from tropical Queensland and the Northern Territory coastal regions — producing abundant small, round, bright orange-red to red berries with sweet-tart, pleasant flavor, and tolerant of salt spray, wind, and sandy coastal soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; excellent salt and wind tolerance makes it outstanding for coastal gardens. Eugenia reinwardtiana (cedar bay cherry or beach cherry) is an Australian native Eugenia shrub or small tree from the tropical beaches, coastal scrub, and dry vine thickets of far north Queensland and the Northern Territory — one of the few Eugenia species native to Australia (most Eugenia, including the commercial Brazilian cherry group, are from the Americas). The plant bears small (1/2-inch), round, bright orange-red to scarlet fruits in clusters along the branches — similar in size and color to Surinam cherry (Eugenia uniflora) but with a different, distinctly sweet-acid, pleasant flavor without the resinous undertone characteristic of Surinam cherry. The fruits are eaten fresh as a snack and used in jams and beverages. Cedar bay cherry is especially valued in coastal tropical gardens for its outstanding tolerance of salt spray, wind, sandy soils, and coastal drought — growing naturally on exposed beaches and coastal headlands. The plant can be grown as a low shrub or allowed to develop into a small tree; it responds well to pruning for hedge use. The attractive, glossy, oval leaves and prolific berry production make it ornamentally pleasing. Very rare in cultivation outside tropical Queensland and the Pacific Islands.
sources: Australian Native Plant Society — Coastal Species
Royal Botanic Gardens Sydney — Australian Eugenia

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring (after soil warms to 60°F+) in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Indian shot is the wild species ancestor of modern ornamental cannas — the starchy rhizomes (achira in South America) have been cultivated as a food crop by Andean indigenous peoples for thousands of years, the seeds are used as natural beads (too hard to eat), and the leaves are used as food wraps.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in warm soil (60°F+) in full sun to part shade in moist, humus-rich soil; in zone 8, mulch heavily in winter or lift and store rhizomes; the large starchy rhizomes can be harvested and eaten cooked. Canna indica (Indian shot or achira) is the wild species of canna from tropical America — the ancestor of modern hybrid ornamental cannas (Canna x generalis) that now dominate garden use worldwide — and a plant with a rich, ancient food history. The starchy rhizomes of Canna indica (and the closely related C. edulis, the 'purple arrowroot' or 'Queensland arrowroot') have been cultivated as a food crop by Andean indigenous peoples for an estimated 4,500–8,000 years, making achira one of the oldest cultivated food plants in the Americas. The large, starchy rhizomes are baked, boiled, or pit-roasted like sweet potatoes; the starch is uniquely large-grained (the largest starch granules of any plant), making achira starch distinctive in culinary applications. The seeds are among the hardest seeds in the plant kingdom (requiring scarification to germinate) and are traditionally used as natural beads and jewelry. As an ornamental, C. indica produces smaller flowers than modern hybrids (typically red or orange-red) but is equally spectacular, with bold, banana-like, green to sometimes bronze-purple leaves and abundant flowering on vigorous, rapidly-spreading rhizomes.
sources: National Center for Genetic Resources — Canna indica
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Canna
International Plant Genetic Resources Institute — Andean Root and Tuber Crops

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, gravelly or sandy soil in full sun; creeping savory is a low-growing, prostrate perennial savory from the Caucasus region with more intense, persistent flavor than summer savory (S. hortensis) — spreading to form a low aromatic mat excellent for rock gardens, herb path edging, and gaps in stone paving.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, dry, gravelly or sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; creeping savory spreads by runners to form a low aromatic mat — excellent for stone paths and rock gardens. Satureja spicigera (creeping savory or Caucasian savory) is a low-growing, spreading perennial from the dry hillsides, steppe meadows, and rocky outcrops of the Caucasus region (Turkey, Georgia, Armenia) — one of the most useful and underplanted creeping herbs for Mediterranean-style gardens. The plant spreads by prostrate stems to form a dense, aromatic mat (2–4 inches tall, 18–36 inches wide) of tiny, needle-like, intensely aromatic leaves with a flavor more persistently savory-peppery than summer savory (S. hortensis), containing high concentrations of carvacrol and thymol. As a culinary herb, creeping savory is used like summer and winter savory — for beans, lamb, poultry, marinades, and herb vinegars. It produces abundant small white-to-pale-lilac flowers in late summer that attract bees and beneficial insects. The mat-forming habit makes creeping savory outstanding for ground-cover use between stepping stones, in herb spirals, and in rock garden crevices where no other herbs would thrive. More cold-hardy than winter savory (S. montana) and consistently more flavorful than annual summer savory.
sources: Herb Society of America — Savory (Satureja species)
University of California Cooperative Extension — Mediterranean Herbs

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy soil in full sun to part shade; Chinese mallow (dong kui zi) is a major leafy vegetable in Chinese traditional cuisine and medicine — the large, round, mallow-family leaves are eaten fresh in salads or cooked like spinach, and the clusters of round, button-like seeds (malva nuts) are used medicinally in Traditional Chinese Medicine.

directions: Direct sow in early spring or fall in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; harvest young leaves frequently to promote continuous leaf production; very easy and fast-growing. Malva verticillata (Chinese mallow, cluster mallow, or dong kui) is a tall, fast-growing cool-season annual or biennial in the mallow family (Malvaceae) — widely grown as a vegetable and medicinal plant in China, Korea, and Japan, but largely unknown in Western vegetable gardens despite being exceptionally easy to grow. The plant produces large, round, slightly lobed, bright-green leaves arranged in whorls up tall stems, with tiny, pale-pink to white flowers clustered tightly in the leaf axils (verticillate = whorled) — the common name 'cluster mallow' refers to this tight clustering of flowers. The young leaves are mild, slightly mucilaginous (like okra, a mallow family relative), tender, and nutritious — eaten raw in salads when small, or cooked like spinach, added to soups, or stir-fried. In Traditional Chinese Medicine, the seeds (dong kui zi — 'mallow seeds') are used as a urinary tonic and to promote milk flow in nursing mothers. The plant is exceptionally productive and self-sows readily; in mild climates it functions as a perennial, re-growing from the base. The curly-leafed form (var. crispa) is especially popular as a leaf vegetable.
sources: Chinese Herbal Medicine Materia Medica — Malva verticillata (Dong Kui Zi)
University of California Cooperative Extension — Asian Vegetables

common_name: Costa Rican Mint Bush (Jamaican Mint Bush, Minty Savory)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Costa Rican mint bush is a tropical perennial shrub from Central America and the Caribbean — with one of the most intensely minty, clean, peppermint-like fragrances of any plant in the Lamiaceae family — used as a culinary herb, a mint tea, and as an ornamental aromatic shrub in tropical and subtropical gardens of zone 9–11.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; pinch regularly to maintain bushy form and encourage leaf production; propagate by stem cuttings. Satureja viminea (Costa Rican mint bush or Jamaican mint bush, also known as Clinopodium vimineum) is a tropical perennial shrubby herb from the mountainous regions of Jamaica, Costa Rica, and Central America — notable for producing one of the most intensely and cleanly 'minty' fragrances of any plant in the Lamiaceae (mint) family, far exceeding true peppermint (Mentha x piperita) in the purity and strength of the mint-eucalyptus aroma. The aromatic compound profile is dominated by pulegone and other menthane-family compounds that create the characteristic cool, clean mint sensation. As a garden plant in zone 9–12, Costa Rican mint bush grows as an upright, multi-branched, woody-based shrub with small, oval, intensely aromatic leaves and small white to lavender flowers attractive to bees and butterflies. The leaves are used as a fresh mint substitute for teas, cocktails (authentic mojito-style drinks in parts of Central America), and culinary uses wherever fresh mint flavor is desired. The plant tolerates light shade and thrives with regular trimming in tropical and subtropical gardens. Hardy to about 28–30°F; useful in containers in zone 8.
sources: Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Tropical Herbs
University of Costa Rica — Ethnobotany of Lamiaceae
